An ordinance was adopted by the City, pursuant to Streets 
and Highways Code Section 36000, et seq., establishing a 
Parking and Business Improvement Area. 
Additional business 
license taxes are imposed within the area to promote retail 
trade activities within that area. 
The ordinance created 
an unsalaried area advisory board to recommend to the Mayor 
and Common Council projects for which the additional taxes 
should be spent. 
These recommendations have been con-
sistently approved over an extended period of time. 
The 
Board members are persons owning businesses and real property 
within the area. 
According to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700 (a) (1) (C), the Board is required to adopt a Conflict 
of Interest Code. 
There is no doubt that the recommenda-
tions of the Board are meant to promote retail trade and 
provide parking within the area, and the members businesses 
may benefit financially from those decisions. 
Under the provisions of the Conflict of Interest Code, the 
board members would be compelled to disqualify themselves 
from making recommendations and the board would cease to 
function with the present membership. 
Government Code 
Section 87101 might provide an exception for disqualification 
if the members participation is "legally required". 
How-
ever, 58 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 670 at page 675 concludes that 
this exception may only be used in isolated situations and 
not where there is a continuing conflict of interest. 
Is 
the exception provided in Government Code Section 87101 
applicable to this board? 
The local law library does not maintain opinions of the 
Fair Political Practices Commission. 
I would appreciate 
it if you could inform me of the cost to get all opinions 
that have been issued by the FPPC as well as the cost for 
those to be issued in the future. 
RALPH 
City Attorney 
RHP:ld 
-2-

sections.question: Several questions are posed regarding income reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act of 1974 and the applicability of Government Code Section 87101 exception for a Parking and Business Improvement Area advisory board whose members own businesses that may benefit from the board's recommendations.
sections.conclusion: According to Government Code Section 82030, income from a business entity located outside the jurisdiction that does not conduct business within the jurisdiction is not reportable. However, income from a business entity located within the City and conducting business within the City appears to be reportable. Regarding the Section 87101 exception, based on 58 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 670, the exception may only be used in isolated situations and not where there is a continuing conflict of interest, suggesting the exception would not apply to this ongoing advisory board.
sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney asks whether certain business income is reportable under the Political Reform Act and whether Government Code Section 87101's 'legally required' exception applies to board members of a Parking and Business Improvement Area advisory board who have financial interests in the area's development.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC indicates that business income is reportable if the business entity is located within and operates within the jurisdiction, but not if located outside the jurisdiction. The Government Code Section 87101 exception for 'legally required' participation does not apply to situations involving continuing conflicts of interest such as an advisory board whose recommendations regularly benefit member businesses.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant OCR artifacts and illegible sections. Multiple questions are posed but the structure is unclear due to formatting issues. The response appears to address at least two substantive questions about income reporting and conflict of interest exceptions. The document's opening and closing are partially illegible, affecting confidence in complete extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Several questions are posed regarding income reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act of 1974 and the applicability of Government Code Section 87101 exception for a Parking and Business Improvement Area advisory board whose members own businesses that may benefit from the board's recommendations.

CONCLUSION: According to Government Code Section 82030, income from a business entity located outside the jurisdiction that does not conduct business within the jurisdiction is not reportable. However, income from a business entity located within the City and conducting business within the City appears to be reportable. Regarding the Section 87101 exception, based on 58 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 670, the exception may only be used in isolated situations and not where there is a continuing conflict of interest, suggesting the exception would not apply to this ongoing advisory board.

An ordinance was adopted by the City, pursuant to Streets and Highways Code Section 36000, et seq., establishing a Parking and Business Improvement Area. Additional business license taxes are imposed within the area to promote retail trade activities within that area. The ordinance created an unsalaried area advisory board to recommend to the Mayor and Common Council projects for which the additional taxes should be spent. These recommendations have been con- sistently approved over an extended period of time. The Board members are persons owning businesses and real property within the area. According to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (1) (C), the Board is required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code. There is no doubt that the recommenda- tions of the Board are meant to promote retail trade and provide parking within the area, and the members businesses may benefit financially from those decisions. Under the provisions of the Conflict of Interest Code, the board members would be compelled to disqualify themselves from making recommendations and the board would cease to function with the present membership. Government Code Section 87101 might provide an exception for disqualification if the members participation is "legally required". How- ever, 58 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 670 at page 675 concludes that this exception may only be used in isolated situations and not where there is a continuing conflict of interest. Is the exception provided in Government Code Section 87101 applicable to this board? The local law library does not maintain opinions of the Fair Political Practices Commission. I would appreciate it if you could inform me of the cost to get all opinions that have been issued by the FPPC as well as the cost for those to be issued in the future. RALPH City Attorney RHP:ld -2-
embedding.summary: A City Attorney requests FPPC guidance on reporting requirements for business income under the Political Reform Act and whether the 'legally required' exception in Government Code Section 87101 permits an advisory board's business-owning members to participate in recommendations that may benefit their own enterprises.
